front: Poem: the tragedy of Pyramus and Thisbe appears in this poem; mulberries turn purple when a man stabs himself after falsely believing a lion ate his beloved
Poem: its fifteenth and final book discusses Pythagoras and metempsychosis and ends with the apotheosis of Julius Caesar, whose soul becomes a comet
Poem: opens with the creation of the universe, followed by the four ages of man (paralleling Hesiod), ending with the Iron Age
Poem: Arthur Golding produced the first complete English translation using 'fourteeners' rather than the original hexameters; Ezra Pound called it the most beautiful book in English
Poem: Ted Hughes wrote Tales from its author, retelling twenty-four stories from this work
Poem: Vertumnus disguises himself as an old lady to gain access to Pomona's orchard; he tells the story of Iphis hanging himself on his beloved's doorway
Poem: contains the ekphrastic tapestry woven by Arachne
Poem: the personification of Fame (fama) lives in a mountain-top brass house with a thousand doorless entrances
Poem: influenced by an earlier work by Nicander of Colophon; its epilogue claims 'the best part of me will be born, immortal, beyond the distant stars'
Poem: the medieval Ovide Moralise is a 70,000-line Old French verse adaptation with ethical commentary; William Caxton published a prose version in 1480
Poem: parodies epic tradition by describing the death of Hercules, then having Alcmena narrate his birth and describe the intercession of Galanthis
Poem: in Eugenides' Middlesex, Cal translates its phrase 'ex ovo omnia' as 'Everything comes out of an egg' and later works at a nightclub as Hermaphroditus
Poem: Ali Smith's Girl Meets Boy reinterprets its myth of Iphis (transformed into a man) in the story of sisters Anthea and Imogen
Poem: Cupid steals a metrical foot from the poet, forcing him to write in elegiac couplets rather than epic hexameters
Poem: Christopher Marlowe translated an elegy from this collection, changing the speaker's lament of impotence into insults toward his partner
Poem: Geoffrey Hill's 'Ovid in the Third Reich' examines Nazi atrocities by juxtaposition with the immorality advocated in this collection
Poem: a didactic work unconventionally written in elegiac couplets; its first two of three sections advise men on picking up women
Poem: imagines the Rape of the Sabine Women as a theatrical production and humorously likens love to military service
Poem: inspired Andreas Capellanus's influential 12th-century work on courtly love
Author: exiled to Tomis near the Black Sea; attributed exile to 'carmen et error' ('a poem and a mistake') in the Tristia
Author: the 'carmen' in 'carmen et error' refers to the Ars Amatoria; the 'error' fuels speculation including a scandal involving Augustus's daughters
Author: the Tristia tells a 'little book' to 'go, but without ornament' and 'enter quietly so my verse won't hurt you'
Author: David Malouf fictionalized this poet's exile in the novella An Imaginary Life
Author: wrote the curse poem Ibis, possibly targeting Augustus, inspired by a poem by Callimachus
Poem: Dido writes a letter asking 'What crime have I committed except to have loved?' before her suicide after Aeneas leaves Carthage
